data.title: Common Error: Formula Types
data.text: Do not confuse empirical, molecular, structural, and displayed formulae. Examiners strictly penalise drawing a structural/skeletal hybrid when a displayed formula (showing every single atom and bond) is explicitly requested.

data.title: Exam Tip: Alphabetical Naming
data.text: Ensure you place substituents in alphabetical order (e.g., ethyl before methyl), completely disregarding numerical prefixes like 'di' or 'tri'.

data.title: Avoid Duplicate Isomers
data.text: Students often draw the same isomer twice simply by bending the carbon chain. Always find the longest continuous chain and name the molecule to check if two drawn structures are actually identical.

data.title: Common Error: Conservation of Atoms
data.text: In cracking equations, ensure the number of carbon AND hydrogen atoms balances exactly. A common mistake is producing too many alkene molecules and exceeding the reactant's initial hydrogen count.

data.title: Be Specific About Environmental Impacts
data.text: When asked for the environmental impact of NOₓ or SO₂, specify that it leads to acid rain. Simply stating 'pollution' or 'bad for the environment' will usually score zero marks.

data.title: Exam Tip: Free Radical Dots
data.text: In propagation steps, the radical produced in step 1 must be a reactant in step 2. You must show the radical dot (•) clearly on the correct atom or fragment (e.g. •CH₃, not CH₃• if the C holds the unpaired electron).

data.title: London Forces and Boiling Points
data.text: When explaining boiling point trends, always mention the 'increased number of electrons' leading to stronger London forces. Just stating 'bigger mass' or 'longer chain' is insufficient for full marks.

data.title: General Formulae Trap
data.text: Remember that cycloalkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ — which is the same as straight-chain alkenes — whereas straight-chain alkanes are CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.

data.title: Naming Check
data.text: When drawing structural isomers, verify your names by tracing the absolute longest continuous carbon chain. For example, '2-propylpropane' does not exist; its longest chain is actually 5 carbons long (a pentane).

data.title: Fractional Distillation Core Principle
data.text: Examiners often ask why fractions separate. You must explicitly state that they have different boiling points due to differing chain lengths and consequently different London force strengths.

data.title: Incomplete Combustion Products
data.text: When writing incomplete combustion equations, read the question carefully to see if it specifically asks for carbon (soot, C) or carbon monoxide (CO) as the required product.

data.title: Defining Carbon Neutrality
data.text: When defining carbon neutrality for biofuels, specify that the CO₂ absorbed by the plant during photosynthesis exactly equals the CO₂ released when the fuel is burned/combusted.

data.title: Exam Trap: Termination Steps
data.text: A frequent mistake in the termination step is combining entirely unrelated radicals, or trying to regenerate a radical. Two radicals must combine to form one stable non-radical molecule.

data.title: Defining the Pi (π) Bond
data.text: You must explicitly mention that a π bond is formed by the 'sideways overlap of adjacent p orbitals' above and below the plane of the σ bond. This exact phrasing is a common mark scheme requirement.

data.title: E/Z Priority Rules
data.text: Do not mix up Z and E. Remember that E is 'Enemies' (highest priority groups on opposite sides) and Z is 'Zame Zide' (same side). Priority is ALWAYS based on atomic number of the directly attached atom.

data.title: Colourless vs Clear
data.text: When describing the reaction of an alkene with bromine water, state that the solution changes from 'orange/brown to colourless'. Stating 'clear' instead of 'colourless' is often highly penalised.

data.title: Precision with Curly Arrows
data.text: Curly arrows must be drawn accurately! An arrow representing electrophilic attack must start exactly at the C=C double bond line and point directly to the electrophilic atom (e.g. the δ+ Br).

data.title: Explaining Major Products
data.text: To get full marks explaining Markovnikov's rule, always explicitly compare carbocation stability (e.g., 'the secondary carbocation intermediate is more stable than the primary carbocation' due to the electron-releasing inductive effect of alkyl groups).
